description:
You are a security engineer who must execute a quick, documented credential-rotation.  
Before running the rotation script you must standardise the shell’s environment so that all audit entries are produced in the same locale and time zone.

Your job in this session:

1. Temporarily change the **current shell’s** time-zone and locale variables so that  
   TZ=UTC  
   LANG=C

2. Immediately after the change, append one single-line audit record to the file  
   /home/user/rotation/rotation_audit.log  
   (create the directory and file if they do not already exist).

3. The audit line must be produced **by a single command** that captures the exact moment of the change and must follow this literal format (spaces and pipe symbols included, no extra characters and no line breaks):

YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC | previous_TZ=<OLD_TZ> | previous_LANG=<OLD_LANG> | new_TZ=UTC | new_LANG=C

• YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC is the current Coordinated Universal Time at the moment you run the command (use `date -u +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S UTC"`).  
• <OLD_TZ> and <OLD_LANG> are the values of the environment variables TZ and LANG **before** you overwrote them. If either variable was not set, write the literal word **unset** in its place.

4. Do **not** modify any system files that require root. Only touch your home directory.

When you are done, `/home/user/rotation/rotation_audit.log` will exist and contain one new line exactly in the format shown above. Automated tests will:

• read the last line of the file,  
• verify that the timestamp portion is in UTC and correctly formatted,  
• check that the `previous_TZ` and `previous_LANG` fields match the values that were set before you started, and  
• confirm that `new_TZ` and `new_LANG` are exactly `UTC` and `C`.

Hint: you should only need two or three commands in total (one `export`, one `date … >> …`).
truth:
Initial shell environment before the agent starts the task:
TZ=Europe/Berlin
LANG=de_DE.UTF-8

Expected resulting file:
/home/user/rotation/rotation_audit.log

Expected single new line REGEX (escaped here for clarity):
^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2} UTC \| previous_TZ=Europe/Berlin \| previous_LANG=de_DE\.UTF-8 \| new_TZ=UTC \| new_LANG=C$

The automated test will:
1. Ensure the file exists.
2. Read the **last** line of the file.
3. Verify it matches the above regular expression strictly.
4. Check that the timestamp (first 19 chars) differs from the test runner’s current UTC time by no more than 120 seconds.
